title: Photography for Instagram: Build a Feed That Stops the Scroll
subtitle: A beginner's mini-course on creating scroll-stopping photos with just your smartphone
description: Learn the core principles of photography composition, lighting, and editing to build a beautiful, consistent Instagram feed from scratch. No expensive camera required — just your phone and a creative eye.
learningOutcomes: By the end of this course, students will be able to apply the rule of thirds and leading lines to compose visually compelling photographs.
By the end of this course, students will be able to identify and use natural light effectively to enhance any photo.
By the end of this course, students will be able to edit photos using a consistent style that strengthens their personal brand on Instagram.
By the end of this course, students will be able to plan and schedule a cohesive Instagram content calendar.

modules.title: The Eye Before the Lens: Composition Fundamentals
modules.description: Great photography starts before you tap the shutter button. In this module you will learn how professional photographers think about framing, balance, and visual flow — and how to apply these principles instantly with any camera.

lessons.title: The Rule of Thirds: Your New Best Friend
lessons.objective: Students will be able to apply the rule of thirds grid to frame subjects in any photo.

lessons.title: Leading Lines and Depth
lessons.objective: Students will be able to identify and use leading lines to draw the viewer's eye through a photograph.

lessons.title: Negative Space and Minimalism
lessons.objective: Students will be able to use negative space to give subjects visual breathing room and create impactful minimalist shots.
keyTopics: sky and water as negative space

quizQuestions.question: Where should the main subject be placed when using the rule of thirds?
options: At one of the four grid intersection points
As close to the edge as possible
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: At one of the four grid intersection points
quizQuestions.explanation: The rule of thirds divides the frame into a 3×3 grid. Placing the subject at one of the four intersection points creates natural visual tension and interest compared to dead-centre placement.

quizQuestions.question: A photographer sees a road disappearing into the horizon. How should they use this element?
options: Avoid it — straight lines are boring
Use it as a leading line to guide the viewer's eye toward the horizon
Crop it out and focus on the foreground only
Place the road in the top-right corner
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Use it as a leading line to guide the viewer's eye toward the horizon
quizQuestions.explanation: Roads, fences, rivers, and staircases are classic leading lines. Placing them so they direct the viewer's gaze into the scene creates depth and visual storytelling.

modules.title: Light Is Everything: Mastering Natural Light
modules.description: Professional photographers say 'photography is painting with light.' This module teaches you how to read, chase, and use natural light to make any subject look stunning — and when to avoid bad light altogether.

lessons.title: Golden Hour vs. Harsh Midday Sun
lessons.objective: Students will be able to distinguish between flattering and unflattering natural light conditions and plan shoots accordingly.

lessons.title: Using Window Light Indoors
lessons.objective: Students will be able to set up an indoor shot using a single window as a professional-quality light source.

quizQuestions.question: Why is golden hour considered the best time for outdoor photography?
options: Because there is the most light available
Because the sun is directly overhead and shadows are minimal
Because the low sun angle creates warm, soft, directional light with long gentle shadows
Because golden hour lasts only a few seconds, making photos rare and valuable
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because the low sun angle creates warm, soft, directional light with long gentle shadows
quizQuestions.explanation: Shortly after sunrise and before sunset, the sun's light travels through more atmosphere, diffusing and warming it. This produces flattering, dimensional light ideal for portraits, landscapes, and product shots.

quizQuestions.question: You want to shoot a flat-lay of your product indoors. What is the best light setup without any equipment?
options: Turn on every lamp in the room for maximum brightness
Shoot directly under a ceiling light for even illumination
Position your product near a large window with indirect daylight and use a white card opposite to fill shadows
Use your phone flash on full power
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Position your product near a large window with indirect daylight and use a white card opposite to fill shadows
quizQuestions.explanation: A large window provides soft, directional natural light. A white card (or white foam board) bounces light back into the shadow side, eliminating harsh shadows without any expensive equipment.

bonusResources.title: VSCO App
bonusResources.description: Industry-standard mobile editing app for creating consistent presets and filters that define your Instagram aesthetic.

bonusResources.title: Understanding Exposure by Bryan Peterson
bonusResources.description: The best beginner book on how aperture, shutter speed, and ISO work together — essential for when you upgrade from a phone camera.

bonusResources.title: Later.com
bonusResources.description: Visual Instagram scheduling tool that lets you preview how your grid will look before publishing — key for maintaining feed consistency.
